name: Customer research / discovery interview ask
icp: Person in your target ICP — could be a current user, a churned user, or a non-user who fits the profile
body:
Hi {{firstName}},

Short one. We're doing customer research at {{yourCompany}} on how {{icpDescription}} actually handle {{topicArea}} day-to-day. You came up because of {{specificReason}}.

I'm not selling anything on this call. I'm trying to understand:
- How you currently handle {{topicArea}}
- Where the process breaks or feels heavy
- What 'good' would look like for you

20 minutes, video off if you'd rather. Happy to share what we learn from the full set of interviews — most people we talk to find the cross-cut more useful than their individual call.

As a thank-you, {{thankYouOffer}}.

If you're up for it, here's my calendar: {{calendarLink}}. Or just reply with two times that work and I'll send an invite.

{{senderFirstName}}

variables.note: How you'd describe their role/situation. e.g. 'engineering managers at series A startups'.
The narrow topic. e.g. 'sprint planning', 'incident response', 'pipeline forecasting'.
Why this person specifically. e.g. 'a referral from {{referrerName}}' or 'your recent post on X.'
e.g. 'a $50 Amazon gift card', 'a copy of the full research summary', 'a free year of {{productName}}.' Drop the line if you don't have one.

notes: The single biggest mistake in research outreach is making it sound like a sales call. Be explicit that it isn't. The promise to share what you learn is the most underrated incentive — it works better than gift cards on senior people. The bullet list of what you'll ask is reassuring; vague 'pick your brain' asks get archived.
